posture: Watch — one fresh, dated NSW-REZ development cleared the relevance bar in the 24 hours to Tuesday morning: a social-licence flashpoint around EnergyCo's sponsorship of the NSW Farmers Association annual conference, which opens on 22 July. Affected landholders and grassroots opponents have branded the sponsorship a 'conflict of interest', with several fee-paying members saying they were dropped from a planned roundtable with EnergyCo representatives; named critics include Walcha High Country Guardians chair Anna Young and a Hunter Transmission Project easement-affected grazier, and the item ties the New England and Hunter transmission fronts to the peak farming body that nominally represents the impacted landholders. The story sits on a paid ACM masthead (The Land) and is carried headline-and-standfirst only, flagged paywall. Everything heavier is a prior-digest carry-forward with no fresh movement in the window and is deduplicated out: Squadron Energy's 16 July decision not to proceed with the 426MW Boorolong wind project inside the New England REZ and the political fight it fed, with the state government branding it the 'first investment victim' of the Coalition's late-June pledge to scrap the zone and its 500kV Bayswater-Walcha line and Squadron rejecting any policy motive; the New England Draft REZ Access Scheme Declaration on exhibition (released 7 July, feedback by 6 August, declaration targeted end-September) after EnergyCo's 14 July webinars, with the EIS expected in the second half of 2026 and energisation targeted for 2032; the refined 1km Muswellbrook-Walcha corridor and its July-September field-investigation program (~150 landholders) against live Walcha High Country Guardians opposition and the 22,000-signature petition; EnergyCo's 15 July South West REZ Transgrid export-upgrade deed (3.56GW); and on Central-West Orana the Port-to-REZ OSOM delivery campaign, the Main Road No. 709 gazettal, and ACEREZ's staged access-track works under stronger heritage controls with the DPHI rock-shelter assessment still open, plus the community roadshow flagged for 11-15 August. A RenewEconomy line rebutting 'wild claims' that firefighters would refuse to attend fires on transmission-hosting properties resolved on inspection to a Victorian VNI West story with no NSW-REZ nexus and was dropped; a reported Essential Energy/Transgrid network-coordination tension could not be tied to a cleanly dated article and was left out rather than sourced loosely. The standing posture is unchanged: the dominant exposures remain the New England political and social-licence contest ahead of the March 2027 state election, and the delivery and heritage risks on Central-West Orana.
summary: One fresh NSW-REZ-relevant item surfaced in the 24-hour window to Tuesday 21 July morning: affected landholders and opposition groups have attacked EnergyCo's sponsorship of the NSW Farmers Association annual conference (opening 22 July) as a 'conflict of interest', with several members saying they were excluded from a planned EnergyCo roundtable. The item links the New England and Hunter transmission fronts and is carried headline-only from a paid ACM masthead. All other active threads — the Squadron Boorolong exit and New England politics, the Access Scheme exhibition, the July-September corridor field investigations and Walcha opposition, the 15 July South West REZ Transgrid deed, and the Central-West Orana OSOM delivery and ACEREZ heritage matters — are prior-digest carry-forwards with no fresh movement and are deduplicated out per the writing rules.

items.headline: Landholders brand EnergyCo's NSW Farmers conference sponsorship a 'conflict of interest' on eve of the event
items.summary: Ahead of the NSW Farmers Association annual conference opening 22 July, affected landholders and grassroots opponents have criticised EnergyCo's sponsorship of the event as a conflict of interest, with several fee-paying members reportedly told they could no longer take part in a planned roundtable with EnergyCo representatives. Named critics include Walcha High Country Guardians chair Anna Young and a Hunter grazier in protracted easement negotiations over the Hunter Transmission Project; EnergyCo is one of several energy-transition bodies sponsoring the event alongside ACEREZ, Essential Energy and Origin Energy.
items.so_what: The framing that a government transmission proponent is 'buying in' with the very peak body meant to represent impacted landholders directly threatens EnergyCo's social licence and hands the opposition a sympathetic, election-relevant narrative across both the New England and Hunter fronts; the roundtable-exclusion claim also risks reinforcing the existing consultation-adequacy grievances (including live EWON scrutiny of Hunter Transmission Project landholder dealings) just as corridor field investigations and the New England Access Scheme exhibition proceed.
